title: Nail Polish on White Cotton
stainChemistry: Classification: Resin/Polymer + Pigment. Nail polish is a nitrocellulose polymer dissolved in ethyl acetate and butyl acetate solvents, with plasticizers (dibutyl phthalate or tosylamide), film-forming resins, and suspended pigment. When the solvents evaporate, the nitrocellulose polymerizes into a hard, glossy film — essentially a thin plastic coating on the fabric. Dan Eisen: nail polish is a difficult stain because it contains a plastic composition with a pigment dye. It imparts stiffness to the fabric and usually remains on the surface. The treatment: acetone dissolves the nitrocellulose polymer. On white cotton, acetone is SAFE (Grade B) and the full bleaching arsenal is available for residual pigment. CRITICAL SAFETY NOTE: Acetone is NEVER safe on acetate or triacetate fabrics — it dissolves these fibers instantly. On COTTON, it's fine.
whyThisWorks: Acetone dissolves the nitrocellulose polymer film — it literally liquifies the plastic coating back to its pre-cured state. Once the polymer film is dissolved, the trapped pigment is freed and can be flushed out. On white cotton, any residual pigment color can be addressed with oxidizing or reducing bleach. Dan Eisen's procedure: petroleum jelly for lubrication, acetone to dissolve, spoon for mechanical action on a glass work surface, then wash. The IRTA report confirms: POG agents also work on nail polish, but acetone is the primary and most effective agent.

spottingProtocol.agent: FIBER CHECK — Confirm cotton, NOT acetate
spottingProtocol.technique: Verify fabric content before introducing acetone
spottingProtocol.instruction: BEFORE ANY TREATMENT: verify the fabric is actually cotton and does NOT contain acetate. Check the care label. If the garment has an acetate lining, protect it — do not let acetone contact the lining. Acetone dissolves acetate and triacetate fibers INSTANTLY. The IRTA report and fiber safety matrix both flag this as a Grade F (NEVER USE) combination. On confirmed cotton, acetone is safe (Grade B). Also check for spandex blends — acetone can degrade elastane with repeated exposure.

spottingProtocol.technique: Physical removal of dried nail polish film
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dan Eisen's procedure: place garment over the upturned base of a glass (provides a firm work surface) with a towel between for absorption. If the nail polish is thick, carefully scrape off the surface film with a spatula. Nail polish usually remains on the surface of the fabric — this is actually good news because most of the polymer can be removed mechanically before chemistry. On cotton, you can scrape firmly — the fiber is strong.
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull spatula, glass (upturned) as work surface, clean towel

spottingProtocol.technique: Dissolve the nitrocellulose polymer — primary chemical treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dan Eisen's procedure: apply a little petroleum jelly (Vaseline) to the stain for lubrication. Add a few drops of acetone or nail polish remover. Carefully rub the area with the round edge of a spoon to break up the stain. Have patience and allow time for the acetone to loosen the stain. The acetone dissolves the nitrocellulose polymer film — you'll see the hard gloss softening and becoming liquid again. Blot with the towel, apply more acetone, work again. Continue until the polish is fully dissolved. On cotton, pure acetone is more effective than nail polish remover (which contains moisturizers that slow dissolution).
spottingProtocol.equipment: Pure acetone, petroleum jelly, spoon, clean towels, glass work surface per Eisen

spottingProtocol.agent: POG agent or amyl acetate — for residual resin
spottingProtocol.technique: Secondary solvent for remaining polymer and plasticizer
spottingProtocol.instruction: After acetone dissolves the bulk polymer, apply POG agent to address any remaining plasticizer or film-forming resin residue. The IRTA report confirms POG agents are effective on nail polish. Amyl acetate is also effective as a follow-up for any resin the acetone didn't fully dissolve. Flush with VDS. Check the towel — if color is still transferring, repeat acetone treatment.
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG agent or amyl acetate, VDS

spottingProtocol.agent: NSD flush + hydrogen peroxide 6% for pigment residue
spottingProtocol.technique: Wetside cleanup and bleaching for residual pigment on white cotton
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush with NSD and water to remove all solvent residue. If pigment color remains after polymer dissolution (common with red and dark polishes), apply hydrogen peroxide 6% with a drop of ammonia. On white cotton, apply steam — heat accelerates bleaching and cotton handles it well. The peroxide breaks down the organic pigment chromophore. For glitter/metallic polishes: the metallic particles are mineral-based and won't respond to bleach — they must be mechanically extracted. Use the spotting board vacuum.
spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD, water gun, H2O2 6%, ammonia 28%, steam gun

spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply dilute acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize alkaline residue from the ammonia step. Flush thoroughly. Air dry and inspect. On white cotton, any residual pigment will be clearly visible. If color remains, Dan Eisen's reducing bleach approach: dissolve sodium hydrosulfite in warm water, apply with Q-tip, rinse within 15 minutes. Reducing bleach is effective on pigment dyes that resist oxidizing bleach.
spottingProtocol.equipment: Acetic acid (1:10), water gun, sodium hydrosulfite (if needed)

spottingProtocol.instruction: Air dry completely. Inspect under strong light. Check for: (1) pigment color residue, (2) stiffness from incomplete polymer dissolution — run your finger over the area, it should feel identical to surrounding fabric. If stiff, repeat acetone treatment. If color remains but texture is normal, repeat bleaching step. Once clean and soft, standard cotton press temperature is safe.

homeSolutions: FIRST: Confirm your fabric is cotton, NOT acetate — acetone dissolves acetate instantly. Check the care label.

Dan Eisen's home method: place fabric over the upturned base of a glass with a towel over it for absorption. Apply a little Vaseline to the stain. Add a few drops of acetone or nail polish remover. Rub gently with the round edge of a spoon. Rinse with detergent and water. Wash the garment.
For colored nail polish residue on white cotton: apply 3% hydrogen peroxide directly to the pigment stain. Let sit 10-15 minutes. Blot. Repeat if needed.
Launder in warm water with regular detergent. Inspect before drying — heat can set any remaining pigment permanently.
materialWarnings: ⚠️ ACETONE + ACETATE = DESTROYED FABRIC. Acetone DISSOLVES acetate and triacetate fibers instantly and completely. Always verify fabric content. If there's an acetate lining, protect it.
⚠️ ACETONE + TRIACETATE = DESTROYED FABRIC. Same as above — triacetate is equally vulnerable.
Acetone is highly FLAMMABLE — no open flames, no ignition sources nearby. Work in a ventilated area.
On cotton, acetone is safe (Grade B) — apply to cloth, blot quickly.
Dan Eisen: acetone can remove surface prints and must be tested on an unexposed area before using.
NEVER use dryer until nail polish is confirmed gone — heat permanently sets any remaining polymer or pigment.
Petroleum jelly (Vaseline) as lubricant prevents mechanical damage during spoon rubbing — Eisen recommends it.
escalation: If nail polish persists after acetone treatment: the formula may be gel-type or contain UV-cured polymers that resist acetone dissolution. Try extended acetone soaking (saturate a cotton pad, place over the stain, cover with plastic wrap to prevent evaporation, wait 15-20 minutes). If gel polish, professional nail salons use specialized removers containing methyl ethyl ketone (MEK) — this is effective but requires professional handling. For extremely stubborn metallic or glitter polishes, mechanical removal with fine-point tweezers may be necessary after acetone softens the film.

professional.name: Pure Acetone
professional.note: Primary nail polish solvent. Dissolves nitrocellulose polymer. NEVER on acetate/triacetate.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG Remover
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson NovaBone POG remover professional spotting
professional.note: IRTA-confirmed effective on nail polish. Secondary to acetone.

professional.name: Amyl Acetate (Banana Oil)
professional.amazonSearchQuery: amyl acetate banana oil spotting solvent
professional.note: Follow-up for residual resin. Safer alternative to acetone near acetate linings.

household.name: 100% Acetone Nail Polish Remover
household.amazonSearchQuery: 100 percent pure acetone nail polish remover
household.note: Pure acetone is more effective than acetone-free removers. Verify cotton fabric first.

household.name: Petroleum Jelly (Vaseline)
household.note: Eisen's recommended lubricant during mechanical nail polish removal.

household.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
household.note: For residual pigment color on white cotton after polymer dissolution.

customerExplanation: Nail polish is essentially a thin plastic film with color pigment inside it. Our treatment uses a solvent that dissolves the plastic back into liquid form — you can think of it as un-painting the nail polish. Once the plastic film is dissolved, we flush out the freed pigment. On white cotton, we can follow up with bleaching for any remaining color, giving us the best possible result.
commonMistakes: Not checking fabric content — using acetone on a garment with an acetate lining or acetate blend destroys the fabric. This is the #1 mistake and it's irreversible. | Expecting instant results — nail polish is a polymerized plastic film. It takes time for acetone to fully penetrate and dissolve. Be patient, work in multiple passes. | Not using lubrication — Eisen recommends Vaseline because rubbing a dry cotton fabric with a spoon creates friction that can damage the fiber. Lubricate first. | Putting garment in dryer before polish is fully dissolved — heat further polymerizes any remaining nitrocellulose, making it permanent.
scienceNote: Nail polish is a nitrocellulose lacquer — the same basic chemistry as early film stock and celluloid. Nitrocellulose is produced by nitrating cellulose with sulfuric/nitric acid, creating a polymer that dissolves in ketones (acetone, MEK) and esters (ethyl acetate, butyl acetate). When these solvents evaporate, the nitrocellulose re-polymerizes into a hard, glossy film. Acetone reverses this polymerization by re-solvating the polymer chains. The IRTA study found that a 90% Soy Gold 2500 / 10% acetone blend was also effective — the Soy Gold provides additional solvency for the plasticizer components while the acetone targets the nitrocellulose directly. Important: the IRTA warns that acetone blends above 10% acetone create RCRA F003 hazardous waste — regulatory compliance matters.
callPro: Fresh nail polish on white cotton is one of the more treatable stain-fiber combinations for home methods — acetone is readily available and cotton is safe. For dried/aged nail polish or gel polish, professional treatment provides extended soak techniques and specialized solvents. For any non-cotton or blended fabric, professional identification and treatment is strongly recommended.
difficulty_factors: Polymerized plastic film requires solvent dissolution — not a simple stain
Acetone safety critical — must verify fabric content
White cotton provides full treatment latitude — favorable fiber
Gel polish and UV-cured formulas are significantly harder than standard polish
solventNote: For PERC shops: PERC alone won't dissolve nail polish polymer — pre-spot with acetone on the board first. For HC shops: same — nail polish requires acetone or amyl acetate pre-spotting. Machine processing handles the residue after polymer dissolution. For wet clean: launder after complete polymer dissolution.
